speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four. The date is February 13, 1996. And the interviewed subject is Abram Asner. You were mentioning that you belonged to a partisan group in the woods. Who was the leader of the partisan group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stankevich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Anton Stankevich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of the partisan group to which you belonged?

speaker: interviewee
text: We changed places. From the beginning it was Alexander Nezsky, then [INAUDIBLE]. [NON-ENGLISH]. What else? Where I was talking about what?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about how you managed to save the two Jews--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and get them into the group.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, we brought them in on this-- finally-- after, they want me apologize to him. Because they made a communist group, who was a member of the communist party. They was ruling everything. They want me. I said, I'm not apologizing to him for anything. He said, if you don't like it, we are four brothers and my wife Libby. That time she wasn't married. Libby, she's the fifth one. We prepare food for-- for the winter. We'll take one third food, and we can go any place we need. We're not-- I'm not bound to that kind of-- so they couldn't do nothing with me. I'm stubborn. And they know they need us. Because we-- we wasn't some kind of pushovers. And we wasn't-- we knew the territory. We were brave. I know that. Everybody know that. And they give in. Always, where I was going, with him-- he was a good leader. As military man, I always keep eye not to be late. I always keep an eye not to be late. I'll-- I'll-- I'll be first. Finally, this one is finished. Then we have a-- a group of pure Russian. One, they want to take over the leadership. One time they call them for a conference, our leader. And they shot on him three bullets. They killed the leader. They want to take over their leadership.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, they call us for a conference, everyone. Everyone from us was going with the mission on there. If everything goes our way, everything was the communist's idea. You know? If everything goes our way, OK, we elect another leader.

speaker: interviewee
text: If not, on the signal, everyone from us have a mission. I'll give example. Me, I was appointed to you. I was sitting with you and talking to you and everything nice. And everyone of us was appointed to another guy. Top guy was sitting two guys with him. On a signal, I pick up the pistol-- bang. All right. Everything was going our way. The leadership was in our favor. And that's-- we appointed another leader from our group, and everything was different.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, [INAUDIBLE] one time, a man, our contact, when the Germans come in one time, they arrest the man. And they told him, do you know where the partisans are? He said, I know where they are, where I can meet-- meet them. Exactly where they are, I don't know. He said, you'll take us down there? He said, yeah, I'll take you down there. Or, you got to come down there and shoot a couple times, then they'll-- they'll come over. And then you'll see them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, that happened this way. They come down there, when they shoot out a couple times, the Germans, informed the Russian. They come in to look on them. They saw Germans. They chickened out. They run away. Finally, we went find out to the lookout. We saw Germans. We killed seven Germans. They killed our one man. And then, they-- it was- we were-- start to laugh on them. They such a big heroes, and they run away from the Germans. They took away all the pistols and the boots. That time I got frozen feet. I took a couple boots. We filled with everything. It was so good. And then we went in another place. We left the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: In another area from the woods, maybe about 25 kilometers in a difference area, no?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where-- where would that have been, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately, White Russian. This was in the Third Reich. This is White Russia crossed the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the closest town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The closest town was Radun.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how many people were there in your group in all?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 300.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were more than 300. Because we were split in a few places. And one time, in 1943, in February, we light up all big farms. Let's make a panic on the Germans and on the population, who was nationalized by the Germans when people was working for them. We came. And the same minute, and same second, that we lighten up at the time maybe an area from 50 kilometers, you know, at night. That is when we light up a barn with grain, with the straw, the fire is big. You can see-- and so, the panic. They give, from the beginning, for the working people, take everything what they need to eat for food and the rest, cattle, pigs, horses, everything, shot. And everything got burned, went down.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you have to do in the partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was mostly intelligence work. I was find out information. I was the-- the leader for the information.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you go about doing your intelligence work?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to ride the horses. And naturally, if you go-- you riding in a village, you don't know anything. First of all, you'll go in with the fastest speed you can up to the village. And you go in. Because you don't know anything what's in the village going on. We, as you're aware, in every village, some people, they got all kind of signals, smoke signals, and blankets, all kind of signals. From far away we used to look with binoculars. Or, mostly, the worst thing to find out where we don't have it. If you take off the fastest you can on the horse, come close to your village and turn right away back. They thought you spotted them. Then they start shooting at you. And you stay for a while with the locals in a hiding place. You see nothing is there, then you ride again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of any of your contacts in the villages?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know their names back then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. We knew-- we know their names. And we know the-- and anytime-- anytime we make around the wood to meet somebody, some kind of very important thing, we never give the date. They never. Doesn't matter what. Doesn't matter how good he is, how come out, he can trust them. We never give it to anybody. Because sometimes, some of the [INAUDIBLE] they put on pressure and they give in. Always we come later. That was our policy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you need information for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Germans, what kind of Germans, what-- what they collecting, everything, all kind of information. Who's spying for them, any kind of information.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you use this information for?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to give to our chief, to our leader. And then sometimes, somebody got to be out of the way. You get them out of the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So besides the occasional assassination, what other kinds of work were the partisans doing in your area?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean? No work, what kind of work would you suggest that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you blowing up any trains or things like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I never. One time they took me to blow up a train. They took me in the-- call me right away back. They [INAUDIBLE]. They didn't let me go. They want me to take with me. My brother used to go to blow up trains and other things like that. I was mostly intelligence. I never been on a train to blow up. Or, other-- one time, we went one time in a village to collect ammunition. I was down there. We was 12 people. That time was another leader took over the leadership. And I been in that strange area, far away from the woods. And we were staying the day on a farm. And who comes in on the farm, we stop them and not let them go back till we-- till we leave. Finally, the Germans find out. It was already at night. No, the night is close, very close. At night, took off the boots, take a rest. Finally, somebody yelled, Germans. We were 12 men. Three left already, because already we thinking to go back home, back to the-- to the-- to the woods. Three left before. And we was nine. And we had a machine gun, with us, with everything. And when I took off the boots, everybody ran out. I was the last one. And I have a coat, a sheepskin. I didn't make it to put them on. And they opened up on us fire, terrible. We answer them with fire. We realized then, we organized [INAUDIBLE]. When they opened on us fire, they were maybe about 80. On my sheepskin, I couldn't make it to put it on. I just left it. And thought it's a man. Most of the bullets went in that sheepskin. And then it was in the front of was a farm. And they fire with bullets. I don't know how they call them in English. The-- they-- they boom. They make a fire, that fire. The-- the barn start to burn. And the smoke cover up. And went out safe. The dense smoke wouldn't cover up, we would never come out down there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in the woods, where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes we used to make like a bunker, sometimes like a dugout. It depend.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long would you stay in one place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too long. And then one time, the Germans collect always the milk and eggs from the farmers. And we decided-- not I decided, the leader, to demolish that dairy. The dairy was in-- in the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In town, Zablocie. On-- it was one man from down there, a Jewish boy. And a girl was from not far from that town. And then they took me. I'm the third one. I don't know nothing about the town. Finally, we walk in-- out three, the first. And we put in a piece of dynamite in the door, in the dairy. We blow it out, the door, and then start to break all the machines and the-- the cream and the stools, everything to mess up the eggs, everything. Then we took with a horse and wagon all the [NON-ENGLISH] everything on the wagon, a pull wagon. And I was going on patrol at that time. One Russian guy comes out. He said, you got enough! I'll go on patrol. I went to sit on the wagon. And the Polish underground, they make an ambush on us. They let through the patrol. And then they open up fire on us. First of all, they killed right away the horse. And when I jumped from the wagon, they hit me. And they hit the guy with the machine gun. And we start to run. And I feel I hit. I said, don't let me down. I said, I'm hit in my leg. Finally, we run away. And we start to cross in another place. The-- and the highway was ambushed down there again. Then we tried to cross in another place. It was ambushed down there again all night. Then finally, we make all kind of go around. We crossed two rivers. We come back, maybe about three hours later. In the morning, in the village, no lights, no nothing. The lights we used to make at that time, I don't know how you call this, split wood, to burn for lights. You take a piece of wood with-- because they don't have any electricity, no kerosene lamps, no nothing. And all kind of bugs down there in that house was. And then they start to look up the wounded people. And I was sitting in the front from my friend. He was-- his name was [INAUDIBLE]. He was with the machine gun. And I see he was shot over here. I was shot in my-- in my leg. And he was shot over here. The bullet come out over here. When I look over here, it's a little bit blood over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I think, you got hit over here? There is the blood over here. And I just look in. It was right over here, a bullet, how close he was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, they took care on us and everything. We come back later in the camp. And I can stay how as long I want. I been two days, and I went back again. Revenge to me was such a beautiful thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you with the partisans in all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two and a half years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened at the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still, it's not-- it's not the end. At the end, we start to prepare food, because we hear already on the news, and we hear the front is coming closer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What month and what year would the front have been coming close to where you were?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the month?

speaker: interviewee
text: The month? We heard already they're shooting in May, and in-- in May and June. Then we start to prepare food. And we start-- and then we come in to bring the [INAUDIBLE] food in-- in case we can't go out. Then one time, one guy comes in. And he said to give a report to the commander. He said, the Russian, the Red Army, the patrol-- he brought in the patrol from the Red Amy to the partisans. When he was talking, we are mostly lots of guys who used to wear the same Russian uniforms, like in the partisans.

speaker: interviewee
text: When my wife was giving them eat, and she [INAUDIBLE] who do you give them to eat now? Finally, she said, are you from the Red Army? He said, yeah. And they praised us. They don't know how to praise.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another thing what happened. After-- we're running out of time, just a little. The leader, our leader, when-- give me a mission. He give me a document. Go far away, in a place. It was the secretary from the Polish underground, to catch him, bring him alive in-- in-- in the woods. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they give me, he give me a paper, everything. When I come in, I got to go by train, by bus, by truck. No, buses wasn't there-- by foot. Finally, I came to [INAUDIBLE], it was right in the beginning the-- from the beginning when the Germans, the Russian walk in. And they used to take prisoners. And I was waiting by the commanding post with my rifle. And I was watching the way they taking the prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one Russian, he tell me, he come to me. He said to me, are you a partisan? I said, yes. Here, them two, they are yours. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took them down there. And I direct them where to go, [NON-ENGLISH]. And I got ahead of him. I come back. I come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean you got rid of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: What shall I-- I kiss them? [LAUGHS] I shot them. That's it. And I come back. They give me another four. They give me another four. I said to the lieutenant, you got to give me some help. You got to give me some help, because they run-- they want to run away. Because it's summertime. And in the field, the grain is growing, rye and wheat, and everything. OK. They give me two men with automatic weapons. When they come, they saw them two dead, they start to run. I shot one. They shot one. And I shot another one. One run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did this happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in-- in July, I think. In July, yeah? Finally, one run away. And I see far away. And I could run like a deer. Far away, I let go after him. I let go after him. I caught him. And I brought him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, he start to beg me. I said, oh, no. Anyway, then-- then I shot three. And then-- but they shot him. They were going back on them two guys talking in Russian. You see the way our partisans working? [LAUGHS] Anyway-- So this-- so this was in July '44?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944, Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I see that guy, what they shot him. He's sitting up and asking for help. I said-- I said to them, he got to be finished. He said, no, he'll going to be finished anyway. I said, he's got to be finished. And I finished him up. I give him one bullet. It wouldn't help. Two-- finally. And they was wondering, the guys, the way the operation-- oh, yeah. When I-- that time was new leadership under the same the-- the [? smoke ?] call us up. And they went the first time with us in-- in a fight.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they come to the leader. And they said, them boys, I never met that kind of brave boy. They're excellent. They can go in any place you want with them to go. They never let you down. Now, you finished? I think I have time for one final question.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think back of the events of the Shoah, and how they affected you and your life afterwards--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's affect. It's affect.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which ways?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sadness. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You know, the worst thing was when my daughter get married. And I would never thought to share the news. [CRYING] Really, really that time, really [INAUDIBLE]. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any message that you would like to give to future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: People who believe the Polish they were good. They don't know the Polish. Some people, our Jewish people, they praising the Polish so high. They don't know him. That's my message. I know him better.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to pause here to take some video tapes of photographs. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you want? This over here first? This is my grandfather from my mother's side with my grandmother. It is Leib, Aryeh Leib Kotler, and Kayla Kotler. This my auntie, two aunties. One is Sora, the other, Esther.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in the middle, my mother. And by the right, my brother, Aaron. In the back from my mother, that's me, Abram. Next to me is Yakov. And then my sister, Rivka, and then my youngest brother, Kusiel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1928, something like that, not exactly, according, the way, the ages. This is the picture from my two brothers, and a neighbor's-- and two neighbor girls. The older brother is Yakov. And the younger brother of mine, is Aaron. And the picture is taken, it must be in 1937, or '36, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother is on the bike?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yakov. Yakov on the bike.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taken in Vilna when my brother was in the army with four friends of his. My brother is on the right-hand side facing the picture, Chaim. He is older than me, a year and a half. The picture is taken, I think, in 1938. 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is taken for my brother, Yakov, in the army. And the picture has been taken in 1937. And that picture is in the Washington Holocaust Museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this picture has been taken for me, Abram Asner, in town [INAUDIBLE]. The picture has been taken, I think, in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It could be '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is taken from-- in Berlin, in 1947, of me and my wife, Libby. On that time, I was-- been a volunteer fireman.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK? This picture, I think, is taken not very long ago, maybe last year. This is of my daughter, Cheryl, and her husband [INAUDIBLE]. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is for my younger daughter, Barbara, and her family, her husband, Jordy, her daughter, Jamie, and her son, Jordan. Anything else?

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think to the-- Jordan's was-- to Jordan's bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years ago, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where do they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In West Bloomfield.

speaker: interviewer
text: West Bloomfield.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is for my daughter, with two--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cheryl.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cheryl with her two daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michelle and Jennifer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Michelle and Jennifer. They live in both in-- they live all in Windsor on Mark Street.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture's taken maybe--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple years ago, I think?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yes

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple years ago. This picture-- [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm ready! [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: I've caught up to speed with you! [LAUGHTER] Like, you know. Abe and Libby, myself and my wife. It was taken on Jamie's bat mitzvah, three years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where do they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: The live in--

speaker: interviewer
text: West Bloomfield.

speaker: interviewee
text: West Bloomfield, Michigan.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're all set. This is the end of the taping. And we used four tapes for this interview.
